topics.title: MySQL Internals — InnoDB Architecture, MVCC & Locking
topics.description: InnoDB is MySQL's default storage engine. It uses a clustered B-Tree index (data stored at PK leaf nodes), a buffer pool (in-memory page cache), redo/undo logs for crash recovery and MVCC. MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) allows readers to see a consistent snapshot without blocking writers. Row-level locking with gap locks prevents phantom reads. Understanding these internals explains why certain queries are fast, why deadlocks happen, and how to tune for production.
topics.story: InnoDB is like a well-organized city library. The buffer pool is the reading room — keep the most-used books (pages) out on tables. The clustered index is the main shelf — books are sorted by ISBN (PK). MVCC is like each reader getting a personal snapshot of the library at the moment they walked in — new books added or removed by others don't affect your current reading session until your transaction ends. Row locks are sticky notes on books — only one writer can have a sticky note at a time, but readers don't need them.
topics.analogy: Buffer pool = RAM cache for disk pages (default 128MB, tune to 70-80% of RAM). Page = 16KB unit of I/O. Clustered index = data stored sorted by PK. Secondary index = pointer to PK (two lookups). MVCC: each row has system_version (created_at TRX_ID) and delete_mark (deleted_at TRX_ID). Readers see rows created before their transaction start and not yet deleted. Undo log = old row versions for snapshot reads. Redo log = crash recovery WAL. Gap lock = lock on a range between rows (prevents phantoms in REPEATABLE READ).
topics.memoryAnchor: Buffer pool: innodb_buffer_pool_size = 70% RAM. Page size: 16KB. Clustered index = PK = data row. Secondary index leaf = PK value + index cols. MVCC: READ COMMITTED = latest committed version per statement. REPEATABLE READ = snapshot at transaction start (default). READ UNCOMMITTED = dirty reads. SERIALIZABLE = range locks. Deadlock: MySQL detects and kills one transaction. innodb_lock_wait_timeout=50. SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS: see deadlocks. Redo log: innodb_redo_log_capacity. autocommit=1 (default).
topics.firstPrinciples: InnoDB pages are 16KB — even a single row read loads 16KB into buffer pool. Clustered index: PK determines physical row order — range scans on PK are sequential I/O (fast), random PK inserts cause page splits (fragmentation). MVCC undo chain: each UPDATE creates a new row version in undo log; old versions retained until no active transaction needs them. Deadlock: transaction A holds lock on row 1, waits for row 2. Transaction B holds lock on row 2, waits for row 1. InnoDB detects the cycle and rolls back the cheaper transaction.
topics.bruteForce: Buffer pool too small: every query causes disk I/O (cold cache). No connection pooling: connection per request, OOM risk. Long transactions: undo log grows unbounded, old row versions accumulate, VACUUM-like cleanup can't run. SELECT without index on 100M rows: reads all 100M rows through buffer pool, evicting useful pages.
topics.optimizedApproach: innodb_buffer_pool_size = 70-80% RAM. Short transactions: commit ASAP, don't hold locks while doing application logic. Use monotonic PKs (AUTO_INCREMENT or UUID v7) to avoid clustered index fragmentation. OPTIMIZE TABLE periodically after bulk deletes. Monitor: SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS for lock waits and deadlocks. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.INNODB_TRX for active long transactions.
topics.whenToUse: Understanding MVCC: when debugging 'why doesn't my query see my update?' (transaction isolation). Gap locks: when seeing unexpected lock wait timeouts on INSERT. Buffer pool tuning: when production queries are slow and disk I/O is high. Deadlock analysis: SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS after deadlock. AUTO_INCREMENT vs UUID: UUID causes page splits and larger index size — use UUID v7 (time-ordered) or keep AUTO_INCREMENT for write-heavy tables.

examples.title: MVCC & Transaction Isolation Levels
examples.code:
-- ── Isolation level demo ─────────────────────────────────────────
-- Default in InnoDB: REPEATABLE READ
SHOW VARIABLES LIKE 'transaction_isolation'; -- REPEATABLE-READ

-- ── REPEATABLE READ (default): consistent snapshot ────────────────
-- Session A:
START TRANSACTION;
SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE id = 1; -- sees balance = 1000

-- Session B (concurrent):
UPDATE accounts SET balance = 2000 WHERE id = 1;
COMMIT;

-- Session A (still in transaction):
SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE id = 1; -- STILL sees 1000 (MVCC snapshot)
COMMIT;
-- After commit: SELECT sees 2000

-- ── READ COMMITTED: sees latest committed per statement ───────────
SET SESSION TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL READ COMMITTED;
START TRANSACTION;
SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE id = 1; -- sees 1000
-- Session B commits balance=2000
SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE id = 1; -- now sees 2000 (non-repeatable read)
COMMIT;

-- ── Phantom read prevention with REPEATABLE READ ─────────────────
-- Session A:
START TRANSACTION;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE status = 'PENDING'; -- returns 5
-- Session B: INSERT INTO orders(status) VALUES('PENDING'); COMMIT;
-- Session A:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE status = 'PENDING'; -- STILL 5 (MVCC)
COMMIT;

-- ── Long transaction problem ──────────────────────────────────────
-- BAD: long transaction holds undo log
START TRANSACTION;
SELECT * FROM submissions; -- pins undo log for entire duration
-- ... application processing for 5 minutes ...
COMMIT; -- undo log retained until here for MVCC

-- GOOD: short transactions
START TRANSACTION;
SELECT * FROM submissions WHERE user_id = 42; -- only what you need
COMMIT; -- release immediately

-- ── Check active long transactions ────────────────────────────────
SELECT trx_id, trx_started, trx_state, trx_query
FROM information_schema.INNODB_TRX
WHERE TIMESTAMPDIFF(SECOND, trx_started, NOW()) > 30; -- transactions > 30 seconds
examples.explanation: REPEATABLE READ: InnoDB takes a snapshot at the first SELECT of a transaction and consistently returns that view. This prevents non-repeatable reads and phantom reads via MVCC (no locking needed for reads). READ COMMITTED: each statement gets a fresh snapshot — allows non-repeatable reads but has less locking overhead (useful for read-heavy analytics). Long transactions prevent InnoDB from purging old undo log entries — grows disk usage and slows the purge thread.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring @Transactional default isolation: REPEATABLE READ (inherits InnoDB default). If two services update the same row concurrently: optimistic locking (@Version in JPA) instead of long transactions. DevLearner: XP increment and streak update in one transaction — keep it < 100ms. Long-running admin seed imports: use batch commits (commit every 1000 rows) not one giant transaction.

examples.title: Deadlock Detection & Prevention
examples.code:
-- ── Classic deadlock scenario ─────────────────────────────────────
-- Deadlock: two transactions waiting for each other's locks

-- Transaction A:
START TRANSACTION;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1; -- locks row 1
-- (pause here)
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2; -- waits for row 2

-- Transaction B (concurrent):
START TRANSACTION;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 50 WHERE id = 2;  -- locks row 2
-- (pause here)
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 50 WHERE id = 1;  -- waits for row 1 ← DEADLOCK

-- MySQL detects cycle → rolls back cheaper transaction → other gets lock
-- ERROR 1213: Deadlock found when trying to get lock; try restarting transaction

-- ── See deadlock details ──────────────────────────────────────────
SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS\G
-- Look for: LATEST DETECTED DEADLOCK section
-- Shows: which transactions, which rows, which locks held/waited

-- ── Prevention strategy 1: always lock in same order ─────────────
-- BOTH transactions lock lower id first:
START TRANSACTION;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1; -- id 1 first
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2; -- id 2 second
COMMIT;
-- Other transaction also locks id 1 first, then id 2 → no cycle possible

-- ── Prevention strategy 2: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE (explicit locking) ─
START TRANSACTION;
-- Lock both rows BEFORE modifying — avoids mid-transaction upgrade
SELECT id FROM accounts WHERE id IN (1, 2) ORDER BY id FOR UPDATE;
-- ORDER BY ensures consistent lock order across transactions
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2;
COMMIT;

-- ── Gap lock: prevents phantom inserts ───────────────────────────
-- In REPEATABLE READ, range queries acquire gap locks:
START TRANSACTION;
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE amount BETWEEN 100 AND 200 FOR UPDATE;
-- Locks the gap between 100 and 200 — no other transaction can INSERT
-- an order with amount in [100, 200] until this transaction commits

-- ── Monitor lock waits ────────────────────────────────────────────
SELECT r.trx_id waiting_trx, r.trx_query waiting_query,
       b.trx_id blocking_trx, b.trx_query blocking_query
FROM information_schema.INNODB_LOCK_WAITS w
JOIN information_schema.INNODB_TRX b ON b.trx_id = w.blocking_trx_id
JOIN information_schema.INNODB_TRX r ON r.trx_id = w.requesting_trx_id;
examples.explanation: Deadlocks happen when transaction A holds lock on X and waits for Y, while transaction B holds Y and waits for X — a cycle. MySQL detects this via wait-for graph and rolls back the transaction with fewer undo log entries. Prevention: always acquire locks in the same order across all transactions. SELECT ... FOR UPDATE acquires row locks immediately, preventing upgrade conflicts later. Gap locks prevent phantom inserts in range queries.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring @Transactional with @Retryable: catch DeadlockLoserDataAccessException and retry (Spring Data does this). DevLearner: user XP update and streak update — sort by user_id in the transaction to prevent deadlock with concurrent requests. Bulk import: single-row transactions instead of bulk to reduce lock surface area.

problems.title: Explain MVCC Snapshot Behavior
problems.description:
Explain InnoDB's MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) and how it handles read consistency.

**Scenario:**
Transaction A reads row X (value=100).
Transaction B updates row X to 200 and commits.
Transaction A reads row X again — still sees 100.

**How MVCC works:**
- Each row has hidden columns: `DB_TRX_ID`, `DB_ROLL_PTR`
- Old versions stored in undo log
- Read view snapshot taken at transaction start
- Consistent reads use snapshot — never blocked by writers

**Key insight:** Writers don't block readers; readers don't block writers. Snapshots are per-transaction (REPEATABLE READ) or per-statement (READ COMMITTED).
problems.hint1: REPEATABLE READ: snapshot is taken at the first SELECT in the transaction.
problems.hint2: Q2 is still inside Session A's transaction — sees the snapshot, not Session B's commit.
problems.hint3: Q3 is after COMMIT — new transaction, fresh snapshot → sees latest committed value.
problems.starterCode:
-- Predict the output:
-- Q1 returns: ?
-- Q2 returns: ? (Session B committed 2000 between Q1 and Q2)
-- Q3 returns: ? (after Session A committed)

-- Explain WHY using MVCC concepts:
-- Answer:
problems.solutionCode:
-- Q1 returns: 1000
-- (Session A reads the current committed value)

-- Q2 returns: 1000
-- REPEATABLE READ: MVCC snapshot taken at Q1 (first read in transaction)
-- Session B's UPDATE committed 2000, but Session A sees its snapshot
-- The undo log retains the old version (1000) for Session A

-- Q3 returns: 2000
-- Session A committed → transaction ended → old snapshot discarded
-- New auto-committed SELECT takes a fresh snapshot
-- Sees the latest committed value: 2000

-- Summary of MVCC behavior:
-- Each transaction sees a consistent view of the DB as of its snapshot timestamp
-- Writers create new row versions in undo log; readers follow the version chain
-- Isolation is achieved WITHOUT locking readers → high concurrency
problems.sampleInput: Txn A: READ(x=100); Txn B: UPDATE x=200 COMMIT; Txn A: READ(x=?)
problems.sampleOutput: 100 (REPEATABLE READ isolation — snapshot from txn start)

problems.title: Buffer Pool Sizing Calculation
problems.description:
Demonstrate InnoDB deadlock detection and resolution.

**Deadlock scenario:**
```
Txn A: LOCK row 1, wait for row 2
Txn B: LOCK row 2, wait for row 1
```

InnoDB detects this cycle and rolls back the transaction with the smaller undo log (cheaper to rollback).

**Key insight:**
- InnoDB has automatic deadlock detection — no need to set lock_wait_timeout for most cases
- Always acquire locks in the same order to prevent deadlocks
- `SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS` shows the last deadlock
- Deadlock victim gets `ERROR 1213: Deadlock found`
problems.hint1: Recommended: 70-80% of total RAM for dedicated MySQL server.
problems.hint2: If buffer pool > working dataset size, hit rate approaches 99%+.
problems.hint3: SHOW STATUS LIKE 'Innodb_buffer_pool%' shows reads vs hits. instances = pool_size / 1GB.
problems.testCases:
Buffer pool: 24GB
Expected hit rate: >99% (entire working set fits in buffer pool)
Monitor command: SHOW STATUS LIKE 'Innodb_buffer_pool_read%'
Instances: 24
problems.sampleInput: Txn A locks row1, Txn B locks row2; both try to lock the other
problems.sampleOutput: One transaction rolled back (ERROR 1213); other proceeds

problems.title: Deadlock Prevention — Lock Ordering
problems.description:
Explain the InnoDB buffer pool and its impact on query performance.

**Key concepts:**
- Buffer pool caches data + index pages in memory
- Default size: 128MB (production: 70-80% of RAM)
- LRU algorithm with young/old sub-lists
- `innodb_buffer_pool_size` is the most important InnoDB config

**Monitoring:**
```sql
SHOW STATUS LIKE 'Innodb_buffer_pool_read%';
-- Innodb_buffer_pool_reads: physical disk reads
-- Innodb_buffer_pool_read_requests: total reads
-- Hit rate = 1 - (reads/read_requests) — should be > 99%
```

**Key insight:** If hit rate < 99%, increase buffer pool. Each page fault = disk I/O = 100x slower than memory.
problems.pattern: Deadlock prevention, lock ordering, SELECT FOR UPDATE
problems.hint1: Always acquire locks in ascending order of account ID.
problems.hint2: Use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE in ORDER BY id to lock both rows before any UPDATE.
problems.hint3: If from_id < to_id, lock from_id first. Otherwise lock to_id first.
problems.testCases: Procedure locks rows in ORDER BY id — concurrent transfers always lock same order, eliminating deadlock cycle
